title: Natural selection
description: Test your knowledge of natural selection!

assessment_items.question_content:
Marine iguanas (*Amblyrhynchus cristatus*) are found only on the Galápagos Islands, which are located off the coast of Ecuador. These lizards are unique in that they forage for food in the ocean. 

Compared to land iguanas, marine iguanas have certain adaptations that allow them to survive in their coastal habitat. These adaptations include a long, flattened tail used for swimming and dense leg bones that give the iguanas stability underwater. 

Scientists believe the land-dwelling ancestors of the marine iguana floated on vegetative debris from Central America to the Galápagos Islands about 4.5 million years ago.

**Based on the information given above, which of the following is most likely true about the ancestry of the marine iguana?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation:
Macleod, Amy, Ariel Rodríguez, Miguel Vences, Pablo Orozco-ter Wengel, Carolina García, Fritz Trillmich, Gabriele Gentile, Adalgisa Caccone, Galo Quezada and Sebastian Steinfartz. 2015. “Hybridization Masks Speciation in the Evolutionary History of the Galápagos Marine Iguana” *Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences* 282(1809). https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2015.0425.

Hugi, Jasmina, and Marcelo R. Sánchez-Villagra. “Life History and Skeletal Adaptations in the Galapagos Marine Iguana (Amblyrhynchus cristatus) as Reconstructed with Bone Histological Data—A Comparative Study of Iguanines” *Journal of Herpetology* 46(3):312-324. https://doi.org/10.1670/11-071.

choices.content: Iguanas with phenotypes that were better suited for swimming were able to exploit a novel food source, so they left more surviving offspring over time.
Iguanas with acquired characteristics for swimming were better able to escape from predators and were therefore more reproductively successful.
The iguanas needed to survive in an ocean environment when they arrived on the Galápagos Islands, so they developed adaptive phenotypes over time.
The ancestral iguana population had many more individuals with adaptations for swimming than individuals without these adaptations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Wheat stem rust (*Puccinia graminis*) is a fungus that can cause disease in wheat plants. The fungus infects a wheat plant by injecting proteins into the plant’s cells. These proteins help the fungus grow and reproduce, harming the plant in the process. 

To prevent losing crops to this disease, farmers around the world grow wheat plants that are resistant to wheat stem rust. Resistant plants make their own proteins that detect the injected proteins of wheat stem rust and then trigger a physiological response. This response prevents the fungus from growing on the plants and causing disease.

In 1999, a new strain of wheat stem rust was discovered in Uganda. This strain, called Ug99, can cause disease on wheat plants that were previously resistant to stem rust. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, Ug99 is a major threat to global wheat production.

**Based on the information above, which of the following is most likely true about the ancestry of wheat stem rust strain Ug99?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Singh, Ravi P., David P. Hodson, Julio Huerta-Espino, Yue Jin, Sridhar Bhavani, Peter Njau, Sybil Herrera-Foessel, et al. 2011. “The Emergence of Ug99 Races of the Stem Rust Fungus is a Threat to World Wheat Production” *Annual Review of Phytopathology* 49(1):465-481. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-phyto-072910-095423.

choices.content: A strain of stem rust developed a mutation that changed the structure of its injected proteins so that they were not detected by resistant wheat plants, allowing the strain to grow and reproduce on these plants.

An acquired trait in a strain of stem rust allowed the strain's injected proteins to overcome the resistance response of wheat plants, so the strain was able to grow and reproduce successfully on these plants.

A strain of stem rust needed to adapt in order to survive in an area with only resistant wheat plants, so the strain developed a mutation that allowed its injected proteins to go undetected in these plants.
An individual stem rust spore evolved novel injected proteins that could inhibit the resistance response of wheat plants, allowing the spore to grow and reproduce on these plants.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lemurs are a type of primate found only on Madagascar, an island off the coast of Africa. In general, each species of lemur occupies a different ecological niche within the island’s varied landscape. 

For example, golden bamboo lemurs live in rainforests in southeastern Madagascar, where they eat mostly bamboo. The bamboo contains toxic compounds, but the golden bamboo lemurs have adapted to tolerate these toxins. Other lemurs, such as the crowned sifaka, live in dry forest areas and mangroves in northwestern Madagascar. Crowned sifakas stay mainly near the tops of trees and eat leaves, flowers, and fruit.

**Based on the information above, which of the following is most likely true about the ancestry of the different lemur species on the island of Madagascar?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation:
Pichon, Claire, Laurent Tarnaud, Françoise Bayart, Annette Hladik, Claude Marcel Hladik, and Bruno Simmen. 2010. “Feeding Ecology of the Crowned Sifaka (Propithecus coronatus) in a Coastal Dry Forest in Northwest Madagascar (SFUM, Antrema)” *Lemur News* 15:43-7. https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00575897.

Glander, Kenneth E., Patricia C. Wright, David S. Seigler, Voara Randrianasolo, and Bodovololona Randrianasolo. 1989. “Consumption of Cyanogenic Bamboo by a Newly Discovered Species of Bamboo Lemur" *American Journal of Primatology* 19(2):119-24. https://doi.org/10.1002/ajp.1350190205.

choices.content: Due to variation in the ancestral lemur population, certain individuals were able to better exploit resources and leave more surviving offspring in novel habitats.
Individuals in the ancestral lemur population acquired advantageous phenotypes that allowed them to find food and reproduce successfully in different habitats.
Due to competition for resources in the ancestral lemur population, some individuals needed to adapt to novel habitats, which led to the formation of new lineages.

An individual from the ancestral lemur population evolved to eat bamboo, and another individual evolved to eat parts of trees. These individuals left more surviving offspring in their respective habitats over time.

assessment_items.question_content:
Common carp (*Cyprinus carpio*) are fish that have small, evenly-spaced scales covering their entire bodies. Fish farmers bred a population of common carp over many generations to have only a few patches of large scales on their bodies. Common carp with this patchy scale phenotype are called mirror carp.

Some of these mirror carp escaped captivity and established a wild population in a local lake. A scientist predicted that individuals in this wild mirror carp population would, over time, evolve to have scales covering their entire body due to selective pressures in the wild.

To study this, the scientist collected phenotypic data on the wild population at four time points, each ten years apart so as to include multiple generations of carp. The scale phenotypes were categorized as either partial cover (scales covering less than $10\%$ of the fish's body), intermediate cover, or full cover (scales covering greater than $90\%$ of the fish's body). These data are shown in the table below.

|| **Percent of fish with different scale cover phenotypes at four time points** ||
Time point | Partial scale cover (mirror) | Intermediate scale cover | Full scale cover
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
1 | $71\%$ | $27\%$ | $2\%$
2 | $52\%$ | $20\%$ | $28\%$ 
3 | $23\%$ | $18\%$ | $59\%$ 
4 | $5\%$ | $7\%$ | $88\%$

**Based on the information given, which of the following graphs best shows the type of selection occurring on the wild carp population?**

[[☃ image 2]]
[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Hubert, Jean-Noël, François Allal, Caroline Hervet, Monique Ravakarivelo, Zsigmond Jeney, Alain Vergnet, René Guyomard, and Marc Vandeputte. 2016. “How Could Fully Scaled Carps Appear in Natural Waters in Madagascar?” *Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences* 283(1837). https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2016.0945.

options.alt: A key indicates that a solid line means before selection, a dashed line means after selection, and a vertical arrow pointing down means selection against.

choices.content: ![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Scale cover on the x-axis. A vertical arrow on the left side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-third of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes a bell-shaped curve that is skewed to the right, with a maximum near the end of the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c5cc061ee5db667eb43c0cfa145f8902.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Scale cover on the x-axis. A vertical arrow in the middle of the graph points down and another vertical arrow on the right side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve that is slightly skewed to the right, with a maximum two-thirds of the way on the x-axis. A horizontal dashed line runs through the middle of the graph.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f57c4e0ace5cf8f5e70345cdb97062ef.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Scale cover on the x-axis. A vertical arrow on the left side of the graph points down and another vertical arrow on the right side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-half of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes a tall, narrow bell-shaped curve with a maximum near one-half of the way on the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff21e984941d533096522fd62c33c226.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Scale cover on the x-axis. A vertical arrow in the center of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-half of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes an M-shaped curve with maxima near each end of the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c61e291196e44988397ff71c585c622c.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
A population of medium ground finches (*Geospiza fortis*) on an island off the coast of South America have variation in beak size. Beak size is an inherited trait in the population. 

The finches use their beaks to break and eat seeds of various sizes. Finches with larger beaks are better at eating larger seeds, while finches with smaller beaks are better at eating smaller seeds. 

A group of researchers studying this population of finches observed that after a severe drought, many of the birds with small beaks died. The drought had caused a decrease in  the number of small seeds found on the island and an increase in the number of large, hard seeds. These large seeds were difficult for the birds with small beaks to eat.

 **Based on the information given, which of the following graphs best shows the type of selection occurring on the finch population after the drought?**

[[☃ image 1]]
[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation:
Grant, Rosemary and Peter Grant. 2003. “What Darwin’s Finches Can Teach Us About the Evolutionary Origin and Regulation of Biodiversity” *BioScience* 53(10):965-75. https://doi.org/10.1641/0006-3568(2003)053[0965:WDFCTU]2.0.CO;2

Grant, Peter R. 2002. “Unpredictable Evolution in a 30-Year Study of Darwin’s Finches” *Science* 296(5568):707–11. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1070315.

options.alt: A key indicates that a solid line means before selection, a dashed line means after selection, and a vertical arrow pointing down means selection against.

A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-third of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes a bell-shaped curve that is skewed to the right, with a maximum near the end of the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/864dcdd2bed38fc51abe5a282cc5e905.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
A population of European eels (*Anguilla anguilla*) in an English river have variation in head width. The eels can have a range of head widths, and the size of an eel's head is correlated with its diet and where it eats along the riverbank. Head width in these eels is an inherited trait. 

European eels travel from the river to the sea to reproduce. Scientists have found that eels with either wide or narrow heads tend to be more successful at this process than eels with intermediate head widths.

**Based on the information given, which of the following graphs shows the type of selection that is likely to occur in the eel population over time?**

[[☃ image 1]]
[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Cucherousset, Julien, Anthony Acou, Simon Blanchet, J. Robert Britton, William R.C. Beaumont, and Rodolphe E. Gozlan. 2011. “Fitness Consequences of Individual Specialisation in Resource Use and Trophic Morphology in European Eels” *Oecologia* 167(1): 75–84. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00442-011-1974-4.

options.alt: A key indicates that a solid line means before selection, a dashed line means after selection, and a vertical arrow pointing down means selection against.

choices.content: ![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Head width on the x-axis. A vertical arrow in the center of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-half of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes an M-shaped curve with maxima near each end of the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/17002defce701a4d1e77ecc9b1140f97.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Head width on the x-axis. A vertical arrow on the left side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-third of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes a bell-shaped curve that is skewed to the right, with a maximum near the end of the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/66ce86fce2b41309b065bf2db6269537.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Head width on the x-axis. A vertical arrow on the left side of the graph points down and another vertical arrow on the right side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve with a maximum one-half of the way on the x-axis. A dashed line makes a tall, narrow bell-shaped curve with a maximum near one-half of the way on the x-axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1a798aa9069c12867ae5214eb87ace6d.svg)

![A graph with percent individuals in population on the y-axis and Head width on the x-axis. A vertical arrow in the middle of the graph points down and another vertical arrow on the right side of the graph points down. A solid line makes a bell-shaped curve that is slightly skewed to the right, with a maximum two-thirds of the way on the x-axis. assessment_items.question_content:
The brown anole (*Anolis sagrei*) is a type of lizard that is native to islands in the Caribbean Sea. These lizards can live in a variety of habitats. Ground-dwelling populations of brown anoles live on lower parts of tree trunks and on the ground. These populations have long legs that help with jumping. Other anole lizard populations live higher up on tree trunks. These populations have short legs that help with perching.

Curly-tailed lizards (*Leiocephalus carinatus*) are a predator of the brown anole. A group of researchers introduced these predatory lizards into the habitat of certain ground-dwelling brown anoles. To avoid these predators, many individuals from the ground-dwelling populations of brown anoles began to move higher into trees.

**Based on the information above, which of the following is the best prediction for what will happen over time to the populations of ground-dwelling brown anoles in areas where curly-tailed lizards were introduced?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation:
Losos, Jonathan B., Thomas W. Schoener, and David A. Spiller. 2004. “Predator-Induced Behaviour Shifts and Natural Selection in Field-Experimental Lizard Populations” *Nature* 432(7016):505-8. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03039 	
					 			
Losos, Jonathan B. 1994. “Integrative Approaches to Evolutionary Ecology: Anolis Lizards as Model Systems" *Annual Review of Ecology and Systematics* 25:467–93. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.es.25.110194.002343.

choices.content: Over many generations, the brown anole populations will likely develop shorter legs as they adapt to living higher in trees.
The brown anole populations will likely develop longer legs over many generations as they adapt to jumping higher up in trees.
Within two or three generations, the brown anoles will likely become extinct due to predation by curly-tailed lizards.
Because of their selective advantage, brown anoles that remain on the ground will likely produce more offspring in each generation than brown anoles that move higher in trees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rock pocket mice (*Chaetodipus intermedius*) in the American Southwest can have light brown fur or dark brown fur. These mice live on a landscape that includes light sand and dark lava rock. Mice that are camouflaged in the landscape are less likely to be caught by owls, which are their main predators. 

Scientists determined that dark brown fur in rock pocket mice is caused by a mutation in a gene called *Mc1r*. Mice with dark brown fur have this mutation, whereas mice with light brown fur do not.

**If a population made up of light and dark mice was moved from an area with light sand and dark rock to an area with only light sand, which of the following describes the most likely result?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Nachman, Michael W., Hopi E. Hoekstra, Susan L. D'Agostino. 2003. “The Genetic Basis of Adaptive Melanism in Pocket Mice” *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences* 100(9):5268-5273. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0431157100.

choices.content: Over many generations, the percentage of the population with the *Mc1r* gene mutation will decrease.
Over many generations, the percentage of the population with the *Mc1r* gene mutation will increase.
Over many generations, a new *Mc1r* gene variant that causes mice to have medium brown fur will arise and become more common in the population.
Over many generations, the percentage of the population with the *Mc1r* gene mutation will remain the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
The pitcher-plant mosquito (*Wyeomyia smithii*) has a genetically-controlled behavioral response to changing seasons: these mosquitos transition from an active period of growth and reproduction in warmer months to diapause, or dormancy, in cooler months. The environmental cue that induces mosquitos to enter diapause is daylength, which gets shorter towards the end of the mosquitos’ active period.

In the habitat range of the pitcher-plant mosquito, regions that are further south tend to have longer periods of warmth than regions that are further north. So, the active period for mosquitos in southern regions lasts later into the year, when the days are shorter. As a result, the daylengths that induce diapause in southern mosquito populations are, on average, shorter than the daylengths that induce diapause in northern mosquito populations.

Researchers have observed that climate change has led to longer periods of warmth in the pitcher-plant mosquito’s northern range.

**Based on the information above, which of the following best predicts the effect of climate change on the northern populations of pitcher-plant mosquitos?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Bradshaw, William E., and Christina M. Holzapfel. 2001. “Genetic Shift in Photoperiodic Response Correlated with Global Warming” *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the USA* 98(25)14509-11. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.241391498.

choices.content: The average daylength that induces diapause in northern mosquitos will decrease because the longer periods of warmth will allow the mosquitos to be active later into the year.
The average daylength that induces diapause in northern mosquitos will increase because the longer periods of warmth will allow the mosquitos to be active later into the year.
The average daylength that induces diapause in northern mosquitos will not change because behaviors cannot be inherited.
The average daylength that induces diapause in northern mosquitos will increase because the negative effects of high heat exposure will lead to more mosquitos entering dormancy earlier in the year.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cane toads (*Rhinella marina*) have a toxin in their skin that can kill their predators. These toads were introduced into Australia in 1935. Since then, Australian populations of cane toads have grown considerably, and the toads are now considered an invasive species.

Australian red-bellied black snakes (*Pseudechis porphyriacus*) eat small animals, including toads. These snakes had not been exposed to cane toads prior to 1935. Researchers predicted that the introduction of cane toads would have placed a selective pressure on Australian snake populations that would favor snakes with inheritable resistance to the cane toad toxin.

To test their prediction, the researchers determined levels of toxin resistance in several populations of red-bellied black snakes that had been exposed to cane toads for different amounts of time. Resistance levels were approximated by measuring how much the snakes’ swimming speed was reduced after being exposed to a non-lethal dose of the cane toad toxin. A smaller reduction in swimming speed meant a higher resistance to the toxin. The data are shown in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the experimental results, which of the following statements about red-bellied black snake evolutionary fitness is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Phillips, Ben L., and Richard Shine. 2006. “An Invasive Species Induces Rapid Adaptive Change in a Native Predator: Cane Toads and Black Snakes in Australia” *Proceedings of the Royal Society B* 273:1545–50. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2006.3479.

options.alt: A graph with Reduction in speed (percent) on the y-axis and Time since first exposure (years) on the x-axis. Data points are plotted from 5 to 60 years. A regression line extends downward from (5, 35) to (60, 13).
options.caption: **Figure 1.** Reduction in swimming speed versus time since first exposure to cane toads for populations of red-bellied black snakes.

choices.content: In areas with cane toads, snakes with higher toxin resistance produced more offspring than snakes with lower resistance.
Snakes that have the ability to swim quickly are more likely to develop toxin resistance than snakes that swim slowly.
The longer a snake population has been exposed to cane toads, the less likely it is to have resistance to cane toad toxin.
Snakes with toxin resistance would be more fit than snakes without toxin resistance if they moved to an area without cane toads.

assessment_items.question_content:
*Babiana ringens* is a flowering plant found in South Africa. *B. ringens* plants have flowers that bloom close to the ground, and a stalk that projects upwards above the flowers. The stalk is not a significant source of photosynthetic carbon for the plant.

Malachite sunbirds (*Nectarina famosa*) use the stalk of *B. ringens* as a perch when gathering nectar from the plant’s flowers. The stalk positions the birds so that their bodies rub the flowers when they reach down with their beaks to gather nectar.

A group of scientists carried out an experiment in which they removed the stalks from *B. ringens* plants early in the plants’ development. When the plants flowered, the researchers counted how many times sunbirds visited the plants with stalks removed compared to plants that still had their stalks present. In addition, the scientists measured the seed set, or the number of seeds produced, for each plant. The results are shown below in Table 1 and Figure 1.

[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**Based on the experimental results, which of the following is the best evolutionary explanation for the presence of a stalk on *B. ringens* plants?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Anderson, Bruce, William W. Cole, and Spencer C.H. Barrett. 2005. “Specialized Bird Perch...” *Nature* 435(7038):41–2. https://doi.org/10.1038/435041a.

options.alt: A whisker bar graph with Seed set per plant on the y-axis. A data column on the x axis labeled Stalk present shows a white circle data point at 87 and a black circle data point at 120. A data column on the x axis labeled Stalk removed shows a white circle data point at 40 and a black circle data point at 70. Each point has error bars pointing up 5 units and down 5 units.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** Average seed set per plant for plants with stalks present or stalks removed. Black and white circles represent different plant populations.

options.alt:
|| **Table 1: Sunbird visits to plants with and without stalks** ||
 Plant type | Number of sunbird visits
:-: | :-: 
Plants with stalks present | $24$
Plants with stalks removed | $11$

choices.content: Plants with stalks attract more pollinators, enabling them to produce more offspring in each generation than plants without stalks.
Plants with stalks are able to allocate more resources from photosynthesis to seed growth, allowing them to produce more seeds per plant than plants without stalks.
Because sunbirds prefer plants without stalks, the presence of a stalk allows *B. ringens* plants to avoid being damaged by sunbirds.
Sunbirds are less likely to eat seeds from plants with stalks, so more seeds from these plants reach reproductive maturity.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tawny owls (*Strix aluco*) in northern Europe live in forested areas that experience snow cover in the winter months. These owls have plumage that comes in two primary variations: gray and brown. An owl’s plumage color is an inherited trait.

Scientists studying a population of tawny owls in Finland used data collection and modeling to identify a correlation between tawny owl winter survival and snow depth. They found that survival probabilities were different for brown versus gray owls. These results are shown in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]

Climate change has led to milder winters in many areas where tawny owls live. The scientists were interested to see how this change in climate might affect the tawny owl study population. So, the scientists collected data on snow depth and the frequency of brown owls in the population from 1981 to 2008 (Figures 2 and 3).

[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ image 3]]

**Based on the data above, which of the following statements about tawny owl evolution is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Karell, Patrik, Kari Ahola, Teuvo Karstinen, Jari Valkama, Jon E. Brommer. 2011. “Climate Change Drives Macroevolution in a Wild Bird” *Nature Communications* 2( 208). https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms1213.

options.alt: A whisker plot with Estimated survival (Phi) on the y-axis and Snow depth (c m) on the x-axis. Gray circles data points are plotted and a regression line extends downward from (0, 0.79) to (25, 0.60). Brown diamond data points are plotted and a regression line extends downward from (0, 0.81) to (25, 0.23). Each point has error bars pointing up and down, although the error bars are generally longer for the diamond points.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** Estimated winter survival probability for gray owls (circles) and brown owls (diamonds) at various snow depths.

options.alt: A graph with Snow depth (c m) on the y-axis and Year on the x-axis. Data points are plotted from the year 1980 through 2010. A regression line extends downward from (1980, 12) to (2008, 2).
options.caption: **Figure 2.** Snow depth in southern Finland from 1981 to 2008, measured during the period of winter most relevant for tawny owl survival.

options.alt: A whisker plot with Frequency of brown on the y-axis and Year on the x-axis. The approximate data points are as follows: (1982, 0.25), (1988, 0.13), (1992, 0.21), (1999, 0.26), (2004, 0.40), and (2010, 0.43). Each point has varying lengths of error bars pointing up and down.
options.caption: **Figure 3.** Frequency of brown owls in the study population from 1981 to 2008.

choices.content: As snow depth decreased over time, brown owls became more common in the population because they were better able to survive and reproduce.
As snow depth decreased over time, gray owls became more common in the population because they were better able to survive and reproduce.
As snow depth increased over time, brown owls became less common in the population because gray owls had a higher probability of survival than brown owls.
As snow depth decreased over time, brown owls became more common because the population needed to adapt to the decreased ability of gray owls to blend in with the environment.
